title: Rootless mariadb with persistent storage via bindmount: permissioned denied
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm having trouble getting a mariadb instance running with rootless podman.


When I run this, I get the following issue:

```
chown: cannot read directory '/var/lib/mysql/data': Permission denied
```

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Make a directory to hold the database 
```
mkdir $HOME/mysql-data
sudo chown -R mysql:mysql mysql-data
```
2. Start the database
```
podman run --security-opt label=disable --rm  -v $HOME/mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql/data:z -e MYSQL_USER=user -e MYSQL_PASSWORD=pass -e MYSQL_DATABASE=db -p 3306:3306 mariadb/server
```

**Describe the results you received:**
```
chown: cannot read directory '/var/lib/mysql/data': Permission denied
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Mariadb container starts with correct permissions. Note
```
sudo chown 100998:100997 mysql-data
```
Fixes the issue

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 1.9.2
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

    name: runc

      runc version 1.0.0-rc10

  swapFree: 8235511808

      Version: |-
        fusermount3 version: 3.9.1
        fuse-overlayfs: version 1.0.0
        FUSE library version 3.9.1
        using FUSE kernel interface version 7.31
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```

```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
podman run --security-opt label=disable --rm  -v $HOME/mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql/data:z -e MYSQL_USER=user -e MYSQL_PASSWORD=pass -e MYSQL_DATABASE=db -p 3306:3306 mariadb/server

No reason to disable SELinux here. And you figured out the solution.  Anything in your homedir is owned by "root" in your container.  So if you want the database to be owned by the mariadb user, then you need to chown it to match the correct user.

Ah, I think I was just confused about the mapping of the uid of the "root" container user to host, I expected it to match the mysql id outside of the container but I'm realizing that was a flawed assumption. I can close

You inspired me to write my third blog today.  If you want to preview it, please send email to dwalsh@redhat.com
I think I cover what you did to figure this out.  BTW Good detective work by you, other then disabling SELinux. :^)
